questions.question: Explain the difference between fork() and exec() system calls. How are they typically used together?
keyPoints: fork() creates a new process by duplicating the calling process
exec() replaces the current process's address space with a new program
fork() returns twice (once in parent with child PID, once in child with 0)
exec() returns only on failure (successful exec never returns)
Typical pattern: fork() creates child, then child calls exec() to run new program
This is the fundamental mechanism for running programs in Unix/Linux

questions.question: Describe the six major categories of system calls with one example for each category.
keyPoints: Process Control: fork, exec, wait, exit - control process lifecycle
File Management: open, read, write, close, lseek - file operations
Device Management: read, write, ioctl, select - device I/O
Information Maintenance: getpid, getuid, getgid, time - system info retrieval
Communication: pipe, socket, msgget, shmget - inter-process communication
Protection: chmod, chown, setuid - access control and permissions

questions.question: What is the purpose of the wait() system call? Explain the difference between wait() and waitpid().
keyPoints: wait() suspends calling process until a child terminates
It returns child's PID and stores termination status
Prevents zombie processes by reaping dead children
waitpid() provides more control: can wait for specific child by PID
waitpid() has WNOHANG option for non-blocking check
waitpid() can wait for children in specific states (stopped, continued)

questions.question: Explain how file descriptors work in Unix/Linux systems. What happens to file descriptors on fork() and exec()?
keyPoints: File descriptor is a small non-negative integer referencing open file table entry
Created by open(), pipe(), socket(), dup() system calls
Each process has its own file descriptor table (copy on fork)
On fork(), child gets copy of parent's file descriptors (sharing underlying file description)
On exec(), file descriptors are preserved unless marked close-on-exec
This enables parent-child sharing of files and pipes for IPC

questions.question: What are the key differences between read() and write() system calls? How do they handle end-of-file and errors?
keyPoints: read() transfers data from file descriptor to buffer
write() transfers data from buffer to file descriptor
Both return number of bytes transferred on success
read() returns 0 on end-of-file (no more data)
Both return -1 on error, setting errno
read() may return less than requested bytes (partial read)
write() may return less than requested on interrupts or disk full

questions.question: Explain the concept of I/O multiplexing. Which system calls are used for this purpose?
keyPoints: I/O multiplexing allows monitoring multiple file descriptors simultaneously
select() is the traditional Unix system call for this purpose
poll() is similar to select but with different interface and no FD_SETSIZE limit
epoll() is Linux-specific with better scalability for large number of FDs
These calls block until at least one descriptor is ready or timeout occurs
Essential for servers handling multiple concurrent connections efficiently

questions.question: Describe the protection mechanism in operating systems. List and explain three protection-related system calls.
keyPoints: Protection ensures controlled access to system resources
chmod() changes file permissions using numeric or symbolic mode
chown() changes file owner (user ID)
setuid() sets the effective user ID of a process for privilege elevation
umask() sets default permission mask for newly created files
These implement discretionary access control (DAC) in Unix systems

questions.question: A process executes the following code: pid_t pid = fork(); if (pid == 0) { // child process execl("/bin/ls", "ls", "-l", NULL); perror("execl failed"); exit(1); } else if (pid > 0) { // parent process int status; waitpid(pid, &status, 0); printf("Child completed with status %d\n", WEXITSTATUS(status)); } else { perror("fork failed"); } Explain in detail what happens at each step of this code, including the state of processes and file descriptors.
keyPoints: fork() creates new child process, returns PID to parent, 0 to child
Parent continues to else if branch, child enters if branch
Child executes execl() which replaces its image with /bin/ls -l program
execl() loads new program into child's address space, maintains same PID
If execl() succeeds, it never returns - ls program runs in child
Parent calls waitpid() blocking until child (ls) terminates
waitpid() captures exit status in status variable
WEXITSTATUS extracts the 8-bit exit code from status
printf prints completion status - typically 0 for successful ls execution
File descriptors: child inherits copies of parent's fd, ls may use stdout (fd 1)
This demonstrates fork-exec-wait pattern fundamental to Unix process creation

questions.question: Consider a server process that needs to handle multiple client connections simultaneously. Explain how the server would use system calls to achieve this efficiently. Include discussion of socket(), bind(), listen(), accept(), select()/poll(), and the process/thread model.
keyPoints: socket() creates a communication endpoint, returns socket file descriptor
bind() associates socket with specific IP address and port number
listen() marks socket as passive, ready to accept connections
accept() blocks waiting for incoming connection, returns new socket for that client
For multiple clients: server can use fork() per connection (process-based)
Or create thread per connection (thread-based model)
Or use I/O multiplexing: select()/poll()/epoll to monitor many sockets
select() allows monitoring multiple sockets in single system call
Server typically uses select() to check which sockets are ready for accept() or read
This prevents blocking on one client while serving others
Modern high-performance servers may use epoll with edge-triggered mode
Trade-offs: process model provides isolation, thread model is lighter weight
I/O multiplexing scales better than creating process/thread per connection

questions.question: Explain the complete lifecycle of a process from creation to termination. Discuss all relevant system calls and the transitions between different states.
keyPoints: Process creation: fork() creates new process with copied address space
Parent gets child's PID, child gets 0 return from fork
Process execution: child typically calls exec() to run new program
exec() loads new program, replaces address space, maintains same PID
Process running: CPU executes instructions in user mode
System calls transition to kernel mode for privileged operations
Process waiting: blocked on I/O, waiting for child (wait()), or sleeping
Process ready: in ready queue waiting for CPU scheduler
Process termination: exit() called explicitly or returns from main()
exit() flushes buffers, closes files, calls atexit handlers
Parent notified via wait()/waitpid() which reaps zombie
If parent dies before reaping: child becomes orphan, adopted by init/systemd
If child exits but parent doesn't wait: child becomes zombie until reaped
Zombie: process terminated but not reaped, only kernel data structures remain
Orphan: parent terminated, child continues with init as new parent
